**Gujarati**[[a]](#cite_note-9) is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") native to the Indian state of [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") and spoken predominantly by the [Gujarati people](/wiki/Gujarati_people "Gujarati people"). Gujarati is descended from [Old Gujarati](/wiki/Old_Western_R%C4%81jasth%C4%81ni "Old Western Rājasthāni") (c. 1100–1500 CE). In India, it is one of the 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") of the Union. It is also the official language in the state of Gujarat, as well as an official language in the union territory of [Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu"). As of 2011, Gujarati is the [6th most widely spoken language in India](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers_in_India "List of languages by number of native speakers in India") by number of native speakers, spoken by 55.5 million speakers which amounts to about 4.5% of the total Indian population.[[1]](#cite_note-census2011-1) It is the [26th most widely spoken language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") in the world by number of native speakers as of 2007.[[9]](#cite_note-Nationalencyklopedin-10)

Gujarati, along with [Meitei](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language") (alias [Manipuri](/wiki/Manipuri_language "Manipuri language")), hold the third place among the fastest growing [languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"), following [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") (first place) and [Kashmiri language](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language") (second place), according to the [2011 census of India](/wiki/2011_census_of_India "2011 census of India").[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Outside of Gujarat, Gujarati is spoken in many other parts of [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") by Gujarati migrants, especially in [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") (mainly in [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi")).[[14]](#cite_note-15) Gujarati is also widely spoken in many countries outside South Asia by the [Gujarati diaspora](/wiki/Gujarati_diaspora "Gujarati diaspora"). In North America, Gujarati is one of the fastest-growing and most widely spoken Indian languages in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (especially in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")) and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) In Europe, Gujaratis form the second largest of the [British South Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") speech communities, and Gujarati is the fourth most commonly spoken language in [the UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s capital, of [London](/wiki/London "London").[[17]](#cite_note-18) Gujarati is also spoken in [Southeast Africa](/wiki/Southeast_Africa "Southeast Africa"), particularly in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia"), and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[2]](#cite_note-e25-2)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Elsewhere, Gujarati is spoken to a lesser extent in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_Eastern "Middle Eastern") countries such as [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates").[[2]](#cite_note-e25-2)[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Upadesamala2.jpg/250px-Upadesamala2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Upadesamala2.jpg)

1666 manuscript of a 6th-century Jain Prakrit text with a 1487 commentary in Old Gujarati

Gujarati (sometimes spelled *Gujerati*, *Gujarathi*, *Guzratee*, *Guujaratee*, *Gujrathi*, and *Gujerathi*)[[2]](#cite_note-e25-2)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2001274-23) is a modern Indo-Aryan (IA) language [evolved](/wiki/Evolutionary_linguistics "Evolutionary linguistics") from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"). The traditional practice is to differentiate the IA languages on the basis of three historical stages:[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2001274-23)

1. Old IA ([Vedic](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") and [Classical](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") Sanskrit)
2. Middle IA (various [Prakrits](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") and [Apabhramshas](/wiki/Apabhramsha "Apabhramsha"))
3. New IA (modern languages such as [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [etc.](/wiki/Et_cetera "Et cetera"))

Another view postulates successive family tree splits, in which Gujarati is assumed to have separated from other IA languages in four stages:[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2003115-24)

1. IA languages split into Northern, Eastern, and Western divisions based on the innovate characteristics such as [plosives](/wiki/Plosive "Plosive") becoming [voiced](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)") in the Northern (Skt. *danta* "[tooth](/wiki/Tooth "Tooth")" > Punj. *dānd*) and [dental](/wiki/Dental_consonant "Dental consonant") and [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") [sibilants](/wiki/Sibilant "Sibilant") merging with the [palatal](/wiki/Palatal "Palatal") in the Eastern (Skt. *sandhya* "[evening](/wiki/Evening "Evening")" > Beng. *śājh*).[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry1997654–655-25)
2. Western, into Central and Southern.
3. Central, in Gujarati/[Rajasthani](/wiki/Rajasthani_languages "Rajasthani languages"), [Western Hindi](/wiki/Western_Hindi "Western Hindi"), and Punjabi/Lahanda/Sindhi, on the basis of innovation of [auxiliary verbs](/wiki/Auxiliary_verb "Auxiliary verb") and [postpositions](/wiki/Postpositions "Postpositions") in Gujarati/Rajasthani.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2001274-23)
4. Gujarati/Rajasthani into Gujarati and Rajasthani through development of such characteristics as auxiliary *ch*- and the [possessive](/wiki/Possession_(linguistics) "Possession (linguistics)") [marker](/wiki/Marker_(linguistics) "Marker (linguistics)") -*n*- during the 15th century.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry1997655-26)

The principal changes from the Middle Indo-Aryan stage are the following:[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2003115-24)

### Phonological changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Phonological changes")]

#### Changes in common with other New Indo-Aryan languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Changes in common with other New Indo-Aryan languages")]

* Reduction of [geminates](/wiki/Consonant_length "Consonant length") to single [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") with lengthening of previous vowel (sometimes with spontaneous nasalisation) (#NIA-1/2)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica1991188-27)
* Loss of final vowels (#NIA-3)
* Lengthening of vowel in -VNC- sequences and consequent nasalisation (#NIA-4)
* Loss of unaccented vowels in non-final positions common (#NIA-5)
* Vowels in direct succession coalesce into long vowels or form diphthongs (#NIA-6)
  + Coalescence of vowels of like quality (#NIA-6a)
  + With unlike vowels, the first vowel is generally dominant (#NIA-6b)
  + aï and aü eventually become ε and ɔ (#NIA-6b-3)
* Retroflextion of lateral approximant: *-l-* > *-ḷ-* (#SD-1c)
* Exception to #NIA-1 when a long vowel follows the geminate and the word is longer than two syllables (#SD-2)

### Morphology and Syntax

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Morphology and Syntax")]

* [Morphological](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)")
  + Reduction in the number of [compounds](/wiki/Compound_(linguistics) "Compound (linguistics)")
  + Merger of the [dual](/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number) "Dual (grammatical number)") with [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural")
  + Replacement of [case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") [affixes](/wiki/Affix "Affix") by postpositions
  + Development of [periphrastic](/wiki/Periphrasis "Periphrasis") [tense](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense")/[voice](/wiki/Grammatical_voice "Grammatical voice")/[mood](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood") constructions
* [Syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax")
  + [Split ergativity](/wiki/Split_ergativity "Split ergativity")
  + More complex [agreement](/wiki/Agreement_(linguistics) "Agreement (linguistics)") system

Gujarati is then customarily divided into the following three historical stages:[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2001274-23)

### Old Gujarati

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Old Gujarati")]

Main article: [Old Gujarati](/wiki/Old_Gujarati "Old Gujarati")

Old Gujarātī (જૂની ગુજરાતી; 1200 CE–1500 CE), which descended from Prakrit and the ancestor of modern Gujarati and Rajasthani,[[35]](#cite_note-Dalby-36) was spoken by the [Gurjars](/wiki/Gurjars "Gurjars"), who were residing and ruling in [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat"), Punjab, [Rajputana](/wiki/Rajputana "Rajputana"), and central India.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) The language was used as a literary language as early as the 12th century. Texts of this era display characteristic Gujarati features, such as direct/oblique noun forms, postpositions, and auxiliary verbs.[[38]](#cite_note-M2-39) It had three [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), as Gujarati does today, and by around the time of 1300 CE, a fairly standardised form of this language emerged. While generally known as Old Gujarati, some scholars prefer the name Old Western Rajasthani, based upon the argument that Gujarati and Rajasthani were not yet distinct. Factoring into this preference was the belief that modern Rajasthani sporadically expressed a neuter gender, based on the incorrect conclusion that the [ũ] that came to be pronounced in some areas for masculine [o] after a [nasal consonant](/wiki/Nasal_stop "Nasal stop") was analogous to Gujarati's neuter [ũ].[[39]](#cite_note-40) A [formal grammar](/wiki/Formal_grammar "Formal grammar"), *Prakrita Vyakarana*, of the precursor to this language, [Gurjar Apabhraṃśa](/wiki/Gurjar_Apabhra%E1%B9%83%C5%9Ba "Gurjar Apabhraṃśa"), was written by the [Jain](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") monk and eminent scholar [Acharya Hemachandra Suri](/wiki/Hemachandra "Hemachandra"), in the reign of [Chaulukya](/wiki/Chaulukya_dynasty "Chaulukya dynasty") king [Jayasimha Siddharaja](/wiki/Jayasimha_Siddharaja "Jayasimha Siddharaja") of [Anhilwara](/wiki/Anhilwara "Anhilwara") (Patan).[[40]](#cite_note-Kothari2014-41)

### Middle Gujarati

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Middle Gujarati")]

[Middle Gujarati](/wiki/Middle_Gujarati "Middle Gujarati") (AD 1500–1800) split off from Rajasthani, and developed the phonemes ɛ and ɔ, the auxiliary stem *ch*-, and the possessive marker -*n*-.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Major phonological changes characteristic of the transition between Old and Middle Gujarati are:[[42]](#cite_note-CardonaSuthar661-43)

* i, u develop to ə in open [syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable")
* [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") əi, əu change to ɛ and ɔ in initial syllables and to e and o elsewhere
* əũ develops to ɔ̃ in initial syllables and to ű in final syllables

These developments would have grammatical consequences. For example, Old Gujarati's instrumental-locative singular in -i was levelled and eliminated, having become the same as Old Gujarati's nominative/accusative singular in -ə.[[42]](#cite_note-CardonaSuthar661-43)

### Modern Gujarati (1800–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern Gujarati (1800–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/A_Page_from_the_Gujarati_translation_of_%27Dabist%C4%81n-i_Maz%C4%81hibm%27_prepared_and_printed_by_Fardunji_Marzban_%281815%29.jpg/250px-A_Page_from_the_Gujarati_translation_of_%27Dabist%C4%81n-i_Maz%C4%81hibm%27_prepared_and_printed_by_Fardunji_Marzban_%281815%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Page_from_the_Gujarati_translation_of_%27Dabist%C4%81n-i_Maz%C4%81hibm%27_prepared_and_printed_by_Fardunji_Marzban_(1815).jpg)

A page from the Gujarati translation of *Dabestan-e Mazaheb* prepared and printed by [Fardunjee Marzban](/wiki/Fardunjee_Marzban "Fardunjee Marzban") (25 December 1815)

A major phonological change was the deletion of final *ə*, such that the modern language has consonant-final words. Grammatically, a new plural marker of -*o* developed.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECardonaSuthar2003661-44) In literature, the third quarter of the 19th century saw a series of milestones for Gujarati, which previously had verse as its dominant mode of literary composition.[[44]](#cite_note-45) In the 1920s, the efforts to standardise Gujarati were carried out.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

## Demographics and distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Demographics and distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Jinnah_Gandhi.jpg/330px-Jinnah_Gandhi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jinnah_Gandhi.jpg)

[Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") and [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") were both native Gujarati speakers[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-story-48) but the former advocated for the use of [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu").

Of the approximately 62 million speakers of Gujarati in 2022, roughly 60 million resided in India, 250,000 in [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), 210,000 in Kenya, and some thousands in Pakistan. Many Gujarati speakers in Pakistan are [shifting](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift") to Urdu;[[2]](#cite_note-e25-2) however, some [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_people "Gujarati people") community leaders in Pakistan claim that there are 3 million Gujarati speakers in Karachi.[[48]](#cite_note-Rehman-2015-49)

[Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") used Gujarati to serve as a medium of literary expression. He helped to inspire a renewal in its literature,[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalby1998237-50) and in 1936 he introduced the current spelling convention at the [Gujarati Literary Society](/wiki/Gujarati_Sahitya_Parishad "Gujarati Sahitya Parishad")'s 12th meeting.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry1997654-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Some [Indo-Mauritians](/wiki/Indo-Mauritians "Indo-Mauritians") and many [Réunion](/wiki/R%C3%A9union "Réunion") islanders are of Gujarati descent and some of them still speak Gujarati.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

A considerable Gujarati-speaking population exists in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), especially in the [New York City Metropolitan Area](/wiki/New_York_City_Metropolitan_Area "New York City Metropolitan Area") and in the [Greater Toronto Area](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area "Greater Toronto Area"), which have over 100,000 speakers and over 75,000 speakers, respectively, but also throughout the major metropolitan areas of the United States and Canada. According to the 2016 census, Gujarati is the fourth most-spoken South Asian language in Toronto after [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language").

The UK has over 200,000 speakers, many of them situated in the London area, especially in North West London (in [Harrow](/wiki/Harrow,_London "Harrow, London") and [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley "Wembley")), but also in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), and in [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester "Leicester"), [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire"), [Bradford](/wiki/Bradford "Bradford") and the former mill towns within [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"). A portion of these numbers consists of [East African](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") Gujaratis who, under increasing discrimination and policies of Africanisation in their newly independent resident countries (especially [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), where [Idi Amin](/wiki/Idi_Amin "Idi Amin") expelled 50,000 Asians), were left with uncertain futures and [citizenships](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship"). Most, with British [passports](/wiki/Passport "Passport"), settled in the UK.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalby1998237-50)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDwyer1995273-54) Gujarati is offered as a [GCSE](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE") subject for students in the UK.

Some Gujarati parents in the diaspora are not comfortable with the possibility that their children will not be fluent in the language.[[54]](#cite_note-lang-55) In a study, 80% of [Malayali](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") parents felt that "Children would be better off with English", compared to 36% of [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_people "Kannada people") parents and only 19% of Gujarati parents.[[54]](#cite_note-lang-55)

Besides being spoken by the [Gujarati people](/wiki/Gujarati_people "Gujarati people"), many non-Gujarati residents of [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") also speak it, among them the [Kutchis](/wiki/Kutch "Kutch") (as a [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language"))[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalby1998237-50) and the [Parsis](/wiki/Parsi_people "Parsi people") (adopted as a [mother tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language")).

### Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Official status")]

See also: [States of India by Gujarati speakers](/wiki/States_of_India_by_Gujarati_speakers "States of India by Gujarati speakers")

Gujarati is one of the twenty-two [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and fourteen [regional languages](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language") of India. It is officially recognised in the state of Gujarat and the union territory of Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu.

Gujarati is recognised and taught as a minority language in the states of [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"), [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), and [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") and the union territory of [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi").[[55]](#cite_note-56)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dialects")]

See also: [Gujarati languages](/wiki/Gujarati_languages "Gujarati languages")

According to British historian and philologist [William Tisdall](/wiki/William_St._Clair_Tisdall "William St. Clair Tisdall"), who was an early scholar of [Gujarati grammar](/wiki/Gujarati_grammar "Gujarati grammar"), three major varieties of Gujarati exist: a standard 'Hindu' dialect, a '[Parsi](/wiki/Parsi "Parsi")' dialect and a '[Muslim](/wiki/Muslim_Gujarati "Muslim Gujarati")' dialect.[[4]](#cite_note-auto1-4)

However, Gujarati has undergone contemporary reclassification with respect to the widespread regional differences in vocabulary and phrasing; notwithstanding the number of poorly attested dialects and regional variations in naming.

* Standard Gujarati: this forms something of a standardised variant of Gujarati across news, education and government. It is also spoken in pockets of [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"). The varieties of it include Mumbai Gujarati, Nagari.
* [Saurashtra](/wiki/Saurashtra_language "Saurashtra language"): spoken primarily by the [Saurashtrians](/wiki/Saurashtra_people "Saurashtra people") who migrated from the [Lata region](/wiki/Lata_(region) "Lata (region)") of present-day [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") to [Southern India](/wiki/South_India "South India") in the Middle Ages. Saurashtra is closely related to Gujarati and the older dialects of [Rajasthani](/wiki/Rajasthani_languages "Rajasthani languages") and [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"). The script of this language is derived from the [Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") and shares similarities with modern-day Gujarati.
* Amdawadi Gujarati: spoken primarily in [Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad") and the surrounding regions, in addition to Bharuch and Surat, where it is colloquially known as 'Surati'. The varieties of it include Ahmedabad Gamadia, Anawla, Brathela, Charotari, Eastern Broach Gujarati, Gramya, Patani, Patidari, Surati, Vadodari.
* Kathiawari: a distinctive variant spoken primarily in the [Kathiawar](/wiki/Kathiawar "Kathiawar") region and subject to significant Sindhi influence. The varieties of it include Bhavnagari, Gohilwadi, Holadi/Halari, Jhalawadi, Sorathi.

Kharwa, Kakari and Tarimuki (Ghisadi) are also often cited as additional varieties of Gujarati.

* Parsi: spoken by the [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") [Parsi](/wiki/Parsi "Parsi") minority. This highly distinctive variety has been subject to considerable lexical influence by [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan"), the [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") Zoroastrian language.
* [Lisan ud-Dawat](/wiki/Lisan_ud-Dawat "Lisan ud-Dawat"): spoken primarily by [Gujarati Muslim](/wiki/Gujarati_Muslim "Gujarati Muslim") [Bohra](/wiki/Sunni_Bohra "Sunni Bohra") communities, it has been subject to greater lexical influence by Arabic and Persian and is written in the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script").

[Kutchi](/wiki/Kutchi_language "Kutchi language") is often referred to as a dialect of Gujarati, but most linguists consider it closer to [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In addition, the [Memoni](/wiki/Memoni_language "Memoni language") is related to Gujarati, albeit distantly.[[2]](#cite_note-e25-2)[[6]](#cite_note-auto-6)

Furthermore, words used by the native languages of areas where the [Gujarati people](/wiki/Gujarati_people "Gujarati people") have become a diaspora community, such as [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") ([Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_Coast "Swahili Coast")), have become loanwords in local dialects of Gujarati.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

The [Linguistic Survey of India](/wiki/Linguistic_Survey_of_India "Linguistic Survey of India") noted nearly two dozen dialects of Gujarati: Standard, Old, Standard Ahmedabad, Standard Broach, Nāgarī, Bombay, Suratī, Anāvla or Bhāṭelā, Eastern Broach, Pārsī, Carotarī, Pāṭīdārī, Vaḍodarī, Gāmaḍiā of Ahmedabad, Paṭanī, Thar and Parkar, Cutch, Kāṭhiyāvāḍī, Musalmān (Vhorāsī and Kharwā), Paṭṇulī, Kākarī, and Tārīmukī or Ghisāḍī.[[57]](#cite_note-58)
A manuscript containing Judeo-Gujarati,[[58]](#cite_note-59) Gujarati written in Hebrew characters, by Rahamim Jacob Kohen is preserved in the Alalay microfilms of the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library")'s [Asia, Pacific and Africa Collections](/wiki/Asia,_Pacific_and_Africa_Collections,_British_Library "Asia, Pacific and Africa Collections, British Library").[[59]](#cite_note-60)

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main articles: [Gujarati alphabet](/wiki/Gujarati_alphabet "Gujarati alphabet") and [Gujarati braille](/wiki/Gujarati_braille "Gujarati braille")

Similar to other [Nāgarī](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB "Nāgarī") writing systems, the Gujarati script is an [abugida](/wiki/Abugida "Abugida"). It is used to write the Gujarati and [Kutchi](/wiki/Kutchi_language "Kutchi language") languages. It is a variant of the [Devanāgarī](/wiki/Devan%C4%81gar%C4%AB "Devanāgarī") script, differentiated by the loss of the characteristic horizontal line running above the letters and by a small number of modifications in the remaining characters.

The Gujarati alphabet, called Kakko, has between 47-48 letters. Many letters of the Kakko highlight the utilization of breath when speaking. For instance, the pronunciation of ક (ka) versus ખ (kha) relies on the placing an emphasis on "kh" for the latter letter.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

### Categorisation and sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Categorisation and sources")]

These are the three general categories of words in modern Indo-Aryan: *tadbhav*, *tatsam*, and loanwords.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

#### Tadbhav

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Tadbhav")]

તદ્ભવ *tadbhava*, "of the nature of that". Gujarati is a modern Indo-Aryan language descended from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") (old Indo-Aryan), and this category pertains exactly to that: words of Sanskritic origin that have demonstratively undergone change over the ages, ending up characteristic of modern Indo-Aryan languages specifically as well as in general. Thus the "that" in "of the nature of that" refers to Sanskrit. They tend to be non-technical, everyday, crucial words; part of the spoken vernacular. Below is a table of a few Gujarati *tadbhav* words and their Old Indo-Aryan sources:

| Old Indo-Aryan | | Gujarati | | Ref |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| I | *ahám* | *hũ* |  | [[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc01773soas_p._44._Entry_992.]-63) |
| falls, slips | *khasati* | *khasvũ* | to move | [[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc11229soas_p._203._Entry_3856.]-64) |
| causes to move | *arpáyati* | *āpvũ* | to give | [[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc01552soas_p._30._Entry_684.]-65) |
| attains to, obtains | *prāpnoti* | *pāmvũ* |  | [[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc212005soas_p._502._Entry_8947.]-66) |
| tiger | *vyāghrá* | *vāgh* |  | [[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc312126soas_p._706._Entry_12193.]-67) |
| equal, alike, level | *samá* | *samũ* | right, sound | [[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc41541soas_p._762._Entry_13173.]-68) |
| all | *sárva* | *sau/sāv* |  | [[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1966[httpsdsaluchicagoeducgi-binphilologicgetobjectplc41616soas_p._766._Entry_13276.]-69) |

#### Tatsam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Tatsam")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Gujarati_language "Special:EditPage/Gujarati language") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

તત્સમ *tatsama*, "same as that". While Sanskrit eventually stopped being spoken vernacularly, in that it changed into [Middle Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan_languages "Middle Indo-Aryan languages"), it was nonetheless standardised and retained as a literary and [liturgical language](/wiki/Liturgical_language "Liturgical language") for long after. This category consists of these borrowed words of (more or less) pure Sanskrit character. They serve to enrich Gujarati and modern Indo-Aryan in its formal, technical, and religious vocabulary. They are recognisable by their Sanskrit inflections and markings; they are thus often treated as a separate grammatical category unto themselves.

| Tatsam | English | Gujarati |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *lekhak* | writer | *lakhnār* |
| *vijetā* | winner | *jītnār* |
| *vikǎsit* | developed | *vikǎselũ* |
| *jāgǎraṇ* | awakening | *jāgvānũ* |

Many old tatsam words have changed their meanings or have had their meanings adopted for modern times. પ્રસારણ *prasāraṇ* means "spreading", but now it is used for "broadcasting". In addition to this are [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism"), often being [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque"). An example is *telephone*, which is [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") for "far talk", translated as દુરભાષ *durbhāṣ*. Most people, though, just use ફોન *phon* and thus neo-Sanskrit has varying degrees of acceptance.

So, while having unique *tadbhav* sets, modern IA languages have a common, higher *tatsam* pool. Also, *tatsam*s and their derived *tadbhav*s can also co-exist in a language; sometimes of no consequence and at other times with differences in meaning:

| Tatsam | | Tadbhav | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *[karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma")* | Work—[Dharmic](/wiki/Dharmic "Dharmic") religious concept of works or deeds whose divine consequences are experienced in this life or the next. | *kām* | work [without any religious connotations]. |
| *kṣetra* | Field—Abstract sense, such as a field of knowledge or activity; *khāngī kṣetra* → private sector. Physical sense, but of higher or special importance; *raṇǎkṣetra* → battlefield. | *khetar* | field [in agricultural sense]. |

What remains are words of foreign origin (*videśī*), as well as words of local origin that cannot be pegged as belonging to any of the three prior categories (*deśaj*). The former consists mainly of [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), and English, with trace elements of [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"). While the phenomenon of English [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") is relatively new, Perso-Arabic has a longer history behind it. Both English and Perso-Arabic influences are quite nationwide phenomena, in a way paralleling *tatsam* as a common vocabulary set or bank. What's more is how, beyond a transposition into general Indo-Aryan, the Perso-Arabic set has also been assimilated in a manner characteristic and relevant to the specific Indo-Aryan language it is being used in, bringing to mind *tadbhav*.

#### Perso-Arabic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Perso-Arabic")]

See also: [Persian language in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Persian_language_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Persian language in the Indian subcontinent")

India was ruled for many centuries by Persian-speaking [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent"), amongst the most notable being the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), and the [Mughal dynasty](/wiki/Mughal_dynasty "Mughal dynasty"). As a consequence Indian languages were changed greatly, with the large scale entry of Persian and its many Arabic loans into the Gujarati lexicon. One fundamental adoption was Persian's conjunction "that", *ke*. Also, while *tatsam* or Sanskrit is etymologically continuous to Gujarati, it is essentially of a differing grammar (or language), and that in comparison while Perso-Arabic is etymologically foreign, it has been in certain instances and to varying degrees grammatically indigenised. Owing to centuries of situation and the end of Persian education and power, (1) Perso-Arabic loans are quite unlikely to be thought of or known as loans, and (2) more importantly, these loans have often been Gujarati-ized. *dāvo* – claim, *fāydo* – benefit, *natījo* – result, and *hamlo* – attack, all carry Gujarati's masculine gender marker, *o*. *khānũ* – compartment, has the neuter *ũ*. Aside from easy slotting with the auxiliary *karvũ*, a few words have made a complete transition of verbification: *kabūlvũ* – to admit (fault), *kharīdvũ* – to buy, *kharǎcvũ* – to spend (money), *gujarvũ* – to pass. The last three are definite part and parcel.

Below is a table displaying a number of these loans. Currently some of the etymologies are being referenced to an Urdu [dictionary](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary") so that Gujarati's singular masculine *o* corresponds to Urdu *ā*, neuter *ũ* groups into *ā* as Urdu has no neuter gender, and Urdu's Persian *z* is not upheld in Gujarati and corresponds to *j* or *jh*. In contrast to modern Persian, the pronunciation of these loans into Gujarati and other Indo-Aryan languages, as well as that of Indian-recited Persian, seems to be in line with Persian spoken in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), perhaps 500 years ago.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199175-70)

#### English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: English")]

With the end of Perso-Arabic inflow, English became the current foreign source of new vocabulary. English had and continues to have a considerable influence over Indian languages. Loanwords include new innovations and concepts, first introduced directly through [British colonial rule](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), and then streaming in on the basis of continued [Anglophone](/wiki/English_language "English language") dominance in the [Republic of India](/wiki/India "India"). Besides the category of new ideas is the category of English words that already have Gujarati counterparts which end up replaced or existed alongside with. The major driving force behind this latter category has to be the continuing role of English in modern India as a language of education, prestige, and mobility. In this way, Indian speech can be sprinkled with English words and expressions, even switches to whole sentences.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199149–50-93) *See [Hinglish](/wiki/Hinglish "Hinglish"), [Code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching")*.

In matters of sound, English [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") consonants map as [retroflexes](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") rather than [dentals](/wiki/Dental_consonant "Dental consonant"). Two new characters were created in Gujarati to represent English /æ/'s and /ɔ/'s. Levels of Gujarati-ization in sound vary. Some words do not go far beyond this basic transpositional rule, and sound much like their English source, while others differ in ways, one of those ways being the carrying of dentals. *See [Indian English](/wiki/Indian_English "Indian English")*.

As English loanwords are a relatively new phenomenon, they adhere to English grammar, as *tatsam* words adhere to Sanskrit. That is not to say that the most basic changes have been underway: many English words are pluralised with Gujarati *o* over English "s". Also, with Gujarati having three genders, genderless English words must take one. Though often inexplicable, gender assignment may follow the same basis as it is expressed in Gujarati: vowel type, and the nature of word meaning.

#### Portuguese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Portuguese")]

The smaller foothold the Portuguese had in wider India had linguistic effects. Gujarati took up a number of words, while elsewhere the influence was great enough to the extent that creole languages came to be (*see [Portuguese India](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India"), [Portuguese-based creole languages in India and Sri Lanka](/wiki/Portuguese-based_creole_languages#India_and_Sri_Lanka "Portuguese-based creole languages")*). Comparatively, the impact of Portuguese has been greater on coastal languages[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199149-94) and their loans tend to be closer to the Portuguese originals.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199173-95) The source dialect of these loans imparts an earlier pronunciation of *ch* as an affricate instead of the current standard of [ʃ].[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199175-70)

### Loans into English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Loans into English")]

*[Bungalow](/wiki/Bungalow "Bungalow")*—

> 1676, from Gujarati *bangalo*, from Hindi *bangla* "low, thatched house," lit. "Bengalese," used elliptically for "house in the Bengal style."[[95]](#cite_note-96)

*[Coolie](/wiki/Coolie "Coolie")*—

> 1598, "name given by Europeans to hired laborers in India and China," from Hindi *quli* "hired servant," probably from *koli*, name of an aboriginal tribe or caste in Gujarat.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

*[Tank](/wiki/Water_tank "Water tank")*—

> c.1616, "pool or lake for irrigation or drinking water," a word originally brought by the Portuguese from India, ult. from Gujarati *tankh* "cistern, underground reservoir for water," Marathi *tanken*, or *tanka* "reservoir of water, tank." Perhaps from Skt. *tadaga-m* "pond, lake pool," and reinforced in later sense of "large artificial container for liquid" (1690) by Port. *tanque* "reservoir," from *estancar* "hold back a current of water," from V.L. \**stanticare* (see *stanch*). But others[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] say the Port. word is the source of the Indian ones.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gujarati_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Gujarati grammar](/wiki/Gujarati_grammar "Gujarati grammar")

Gujarati is a head-final, or left-[branching](/wiki/Branching_(linguistics) "Branching (linguistics)") language. [Adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") precede [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), [direct objects](/wiki/Direct_object "Direct object") come before [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb"), and there are [postpositions](/wiki/Postposition "Postposition"). The [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") of Gujarati is [SOV](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb"), and there are three [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") and two [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number").[[98]](#cite_note-99) There are no [definite](/wiki/Definite_article "Definite article") or [indefinite articles](/wiki/Indefinite_article "Indefinite article"). A verb is expressed with its [verbal root](/wiki/Root_(linguistics) "Root (linguistics)") followed by [suffixes](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") marking [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") and [agreement](/wiki/Agreement_(linguistics) "Agreement (linguistics)") in what is called a main form, with a possible proceeding [auxiliary](/wiki/Auxiliary_verb "Auxiliary verb") form derived from *to be*, marking [tense](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense") and [mood](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood"), and also showing agreement. [Causatives](/wiki/Causative "Causative") (up to double) and passives have a morphological basis.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistry2001276–277-100)

:   જગ પ્રસિદ્ધ દાંડી કૂચ પછી ગાંધીજીએ અહીં આંબાના વૃક્ષ નીચે ખજૂરી નાં છટિયાંની એક ઝૂંપડીમાં તા.૧૪-૪-૧૯૩૦ થી તા.૪-૫-૧૯૩૦ સુધી નિવાસ કર્યો હતો. દાંડીમાં છઠ્ઠી એપ્રિલે શરૂ કરેલી નિમક કાનૂન (મીઠાના સત્યાગ્રહ) ભંગની લડતને તેમણે અહીંથી વેગ આપી દેશ વ્યાપી બનાવી હતી. અહીંથી જ તેમણે ધરાસણાના મીઠાના અગરો તરફ કૂચ કરવાનો પોતાનો સંકલ્પ બ્રિટિશ વાઈસરૉયને પત્ર લખીને જણાવ્યો હતો.

:   તા.૪ થી મે ૧૯૩૦ની રાતના બાર વાગ્યા પછી આ સ્થળેથી બ્રિટિશ સરકારે તેમની ધરપકડ કરી હતી.

[Transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") ([IAST](/wiki/International_Alphabet_of_Sanskrit_Transliteration "International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration"))—

:   *gāndhījīnī jhūmpḍī-karāḍī*

:   *jag prasiddh dāṇḍī kūc pachī gāndhījīe ahī̃ āmbānā vrukṣ nīce khajūrī nā̃ chaṭiyānnī ek jhūmpḍīmā̃ tā.14-4-1930 thī tā.4-5-1930 sudhī nivās karyo hato. dāṇḍīmā̃ chaṭhṭhī eprile śarū karelī nimak kānūn (mīṭhānā satyāgraha) bhaṅgnī laḍatne temṇe ahīnthī veg āpī deś vyāpī banāvī hatī. ahīnthī ja temṇe dharāsṇānā mīṭhānā agro taraph kūc karvāno potāno saṅkalp briṭiś vāīsarôyane patra lakhīne jaṇāvyo hato.*

:   *tā.4thī me 1930nī rātnā bār vāgyā pachī ā sthaḷethī briṭiś sarkāre temnī dharapkaḍ karī hatī.*

[Translation](/wiki/Translation "Translation")—

:   Gandhiji's hut-Karadi

:   After the world-famous [Dandi March](/wiki/Salt_Satyagraha "Salt Satyagraha") Gandhiji resided here in a [date palm](/wiki/Date_palm "Date palm") [bark](/wiki/Bark_(botany) "Bark (botany)") hut underneath a/the [mango tree](/wiki/Mango_tree "Mango tree"), from 14-4-1930 to 4-5-1930. From here he gave speed to and spread country-wide the anti-Salt Law struggle, started in Dandi on 6 April. From *here*, writing in a letter, he notified the British Viceroy of his resolve of marching towards the [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt") [mounds](/wiki/Mound "Mound") of Dharasana.

:   The British government arrested him at this location, after twelve o'clock on the night of 4 May 1930.

**Translation** (provided at location)—

:   Gandhiji's hut-Karadi

:   Here under the mango tree in the hut made of palm leaves (khajoori) Gandhiji stayed from 14-4-1930 to 4-5-1930 after the world famous Dandi march. From here he gave impetus to the civil disobedience movement for breaking the salt act started on 6 April at Dandi and turned it into a nationwide movement. It was also from this place that he wrote a letter to the British viceroy expressing his firm resolve to march to the salt works at Dharasana.

:   This is the place from where he was arrested by the British government after midnight on 4 May 1930.
